1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, the entire monoidal scaffolding for (3) is already
88	available abstractly, and the project's task is to \emph{instantiate} it rather
89	than to hand-assemble the coherence data. Two facts drive this. First, the
90	category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a
91	presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric) monoidal category in
92	Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)), with the sectionwise
93	relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\); this applies
94	verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second,
95	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\) --- associator,
100	left/right unitors, braiding, and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
101	naturalities) derived. Sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the
102	localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class
103	\(W = J.W\), so the monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is
104	\emph{free} from this API once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is supplied.
105	
106	Consequently the only genuinely-new obligation is the single instance
107	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the sheafification localizer on
108	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\). Its two fields are the
109	left/right whiskering-stability statements \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(F \triangleleft
110	g)\) and \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(g \triangleright F)\) for an \emph{arbitrary}
111	module \(F\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), and these are flatness-free: a
112	\(J.W\)-morphism is a stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x =
113	\mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an isomorphism (tensoring with an
114	isomorphism preserves isomorphisms --- no flatness, no local triviality). The
115	load-bearing half is the local-injectivity statement
116	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, whose stalkwise proof needs two
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)-level ingredients that are Mathlib-absent at the
118	pinned commit (the module-level stalk characterisation of \(J.W\), and the
119	commutation of the stalk with the relative tensor); this is the
120	\emph{sole remaining open obligation} for the whole group-law engine.
121	
122	With \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) in hand, the substrate \(\otimes_X\) carries the
123	associator, unitors and braiding from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and the
124	group law assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units
125	group is the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as
126	Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
127	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\). The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse
130	existentially. The geometric compatibility isomorphism
131	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any
132	separate tensor-inverse lemma are \emph{off} this critical path; see also
133	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

279	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
280	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
281	
282	\begin{definition}
283	
284	\leanok
285	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
286	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
288	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
289	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
290	  \[
291	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
292	  \]
293	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
294	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
295	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
296	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
297	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
298	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
299	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
300	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
301	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
302	  canonical compatibility
303	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
304	     \;\cong\;
305	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
306	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
307	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
308	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
309	
310	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
311	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
312	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
313	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
314	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
315	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
316	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
317	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
318	\end{definition}
319	
320	\begin{lemma}
321	
322	\leanok
323	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
324	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
325	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
326	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
327	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
328	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
329	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
330	  \[
331	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
332	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
333	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
334	  \]
335	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
336	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
337	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
338	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
339	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
340	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
341	  \[
342	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
343	    \qquad
344	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
345	  \]
346	  the associator
347	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
348	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
349	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
350	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
351	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
352	\end{lemma}
353	
354	\begin{proof}
355	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
356	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
357	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
358	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
359	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
360	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
361	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
362	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
363	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
364	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
365	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
366	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
367	\end{proof}
368	
369	\begin{remark}
370	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
371	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
372	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
373	        lem:whisker_of_W}
374	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
375	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
377	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
378	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
379	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
380	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
381	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
382	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
383	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
384	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
385	  coherence is then derived by the API.
386	
387	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
388	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
389	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
390	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
391	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
392	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
393	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
394	  fixed-base presheaves in
395	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
396	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
397	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
398	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
399	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
400	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
401	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
402	
403	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
404	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
405	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
406	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
407	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
408	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
409	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
410	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
411	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
412	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
413	  none of them is built from the off-path restriction-compatibility isomorphism
414	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
415	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
416	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
417	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
418	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
419	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
420	\end{remark}

511	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
512	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}

514	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
515	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
516	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
517	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
518	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
519	  morphism
520	  \[
521	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
522	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
523	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
524	  \]
525	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
526	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
527	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
528	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
529	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
530	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
531	\end{lemma}
532	
533	\begin{proof}
534	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
535	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
536	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
537	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
538	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
539	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
540	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
541	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
542	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
543	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
544	  isomorphism}
545	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
546	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
547	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
548	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
549	  therefore extension of scalars along
550	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
551	
552	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
553	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
554	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
555	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
556	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
557	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
558	  the abstract pullback functor.
559	
560	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
561	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
562	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
563	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
564	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
565	   \;\cong\;
566	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
567	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
568	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
569	  comparison
570	  \[
571	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
572	      \;\longrightarrow\;
573	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
574	      \;\otimes\;
575	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
576	  \]
577	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
578	
579	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
580	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
581	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
582	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
583	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent

586	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
587	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
588	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
589	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
590	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
591	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
592	  coherence isomorphisms
593	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
594	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained

596	\end{proof}

625	\begin{lemma}

627	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
628	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
629	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
630	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
631	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
632	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
633	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
634	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
635	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
636	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
637	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
638	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
639	  left-whiskered morphism
640	  \[
641	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
642	  \]
643	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
644	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
645	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
646	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
647	
648	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
649	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
650	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
651	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
652	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
653	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
654	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
655	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
656	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
657	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
658	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
659	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
660	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
661	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
662	\end{lemma}
663	
664	\begin{proof}
665	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
666	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
667	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
668	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
669	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
670	
671	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
672	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
673	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
674	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
675	  \[
676	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
677	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
678	  \]
679	
680	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
681	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
682	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
683	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
684	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
685	
686	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
687	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
688	  linear maps (Mathlib
689	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
690	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
691	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
692	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
693	
694	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
695	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
696	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
697	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
698	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
699	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
700	\end{proof}

748	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
749	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
750	
751	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
752	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
753	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
754	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
755	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
756	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
757	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
758	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
759	
760	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
761	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
762	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
763	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
764	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /
765	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). On top of
766	the locally-trivial-scoped associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
767	\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (all
768	either already proved or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled), it needs only the
769	restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
770	dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}; it uses \emph{no}
771	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
772	\(\mathtt{Skeleton}\), and in particular never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation
773	(d.2). The \textbf{fallback} tier is the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
774	\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)
775	(this section); it is deferred because its load-bearing whiskering field
776	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, for an arbitrary whisker object, needs
777	(d.2) --- the stalk commuting with the relative tensor over a \emph{varying} ring, a
778	multi-iteration, Mathlib-absent ingredient --- and is retained only for the full
779	monoidal generality on arbitrary modules, which the group law does not consume. The
780	load-bearing local-injectivity half of \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is thus the sole
781	remaining open obligation of the \emph{fallback} tier only.

869	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
870	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
871	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
872	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
873	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)

875	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
876	  On such a \(V\):

879	      gives a canonical isomorphism

888	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
889	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
890	  \end{itemize}

893	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
894	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
895	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
896	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
897	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
898	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level

900	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.

903	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
904	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
905	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
906	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
907	
908	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
909	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
910	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
911	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
912	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
913	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
914	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
915	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
916	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
917	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
918	  \[
919	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
920	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
921	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .

924	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
925	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
926	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).

929	  pinned commit:
930	  \begin{enumerate}

939	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
940	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
941	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure

1598	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1599	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1600	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1601	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1602	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1603	  \[
1604	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1605	  \]
1606	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1607	  abelian-group structure with addition
1608	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1609	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1610	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1611	  inverse
1612	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1613	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1614	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1615	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1616	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1617	  map
1618	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1619	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1620	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1621	\end{theorem}

1624	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1625	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1626	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1627	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1628	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1629	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1630	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1631	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1632	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1633	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1634	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1635	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1636	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1637	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1638	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1639	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1640	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1641	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1642	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1643	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1644	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1645	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1646	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1647	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1648	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1649	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1650	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1651	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1652	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1653	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1654	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1655	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1656	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1657	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1658	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1659	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1660	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1661	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1662	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1663	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1664	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1665	
1666	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1667	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1668	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1669	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1670	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1671	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1672	
1673	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1674	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1675	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1676	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1677	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1678	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1679	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1680	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1681	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1682	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1683	\end{proof}
